text:p.text:span: Not collated. P. Steinkeller and J. Black both tried to collate the­ original but the tablet could not be located in Baghdad.
saying (the words) of Aradmu, your servant:
Your Majesty, the far-flung territories that have been given to you ­to rule—
they are obedient, and of one mind.
You are the rightful shepherd of Åulgi’s people, numerous as blades of grass,
the god of mortals/the population, of the lower and the upper (lands),
The far-flung peoples, numerous as blades of­ grass, from the mighty waters of the Tigris and Euphrates,
… of the Tigris, that my lord has
Whatever you, my king, order me to do, I will do.
. The line is damaged and without the opportunity for collation one can only speculate on the proper reading. J. J. van Dijk transliterated it as:
but this seems improbable. One could also restoration the broken section as
. In defense of my reading see­
, “(and) have given the far-flung lands to him to rule,” and
åti Anu u Enlil­ ana b
, “their troops are as numerous as blades of grass, their offspring far-flung.” The sign before
may have been erased. The­ most probable reading would be
but the remains are too short­ for this sign. Note that
(if correctly read) is an epithet of Nur-Adad and Sin-iddinam.
The syntax of the line is problematical: I take it to be a complex anticipatory genitive:
. The interpretation of the verbal form
is conjectural. Another possibility is to­ view this as a variant of
, “to present the matter,” ­commonly found in Ur III protocols of legal proceedings and in the­ Ibbi-Sin correspondence. See p.
. The final verb is not clear. One could read
. Two of these three place and “ethnic” names, Mari and Gutium, are known from Ur III texts; the first requires no comment:
a. Gutium. This geographical/ethnic name is almost always written as
from Sargonic through Old Babylonian times. In the time frame of interest to us it is known from the
(155), as well as from various royal hymns of Ur III kings (
Lamentation over the Destruction of Sumer and Ur

. All of these are, of course, known only from Old Babylonian versions and may not reflect Ur III writing conventions. It is not attested in any Ur III document but occurs in Arad-Nanna’s dedication to
u-Sin, where it is written as
in an unpublished Ur III clay cylinder with a copy of the
(courtesy of M. Civil). An Ur III
tablet copy of a monumental inscription of Ur-Namma preserves

office:text.text:p: b. Rapiqum, whose precise location has not been determined, is not attested before Old Babylonian times. It is generally agreed that it was situated on the Euphrates, either close to Ramadi or to Falluga (Charpin 1999: 95). Rapiqum was strategically important in OB times but there is not a single reference to a place by that name in Ur III or earlier documents; this suggests that we are dealing here with an anachronism.

text:p.text:span: 102 n. 27, proposed that the Ur III place name

. This is highly improbable for philological as well as geographical reasons. I know of no other Ur III example in which final /ki/ could be read in such a fashion in a place name, and the few occurrences of
do not favor an Euphratean setting but rather point to a trans-
Tigridian location. The GN occurs only three times and in two of the three it associated with other localities that lay beyond the Tigris. Thus in
are listed together with those from places such as I
um, Arman, Tiran, Hamazi, and Karhar, all of which lay in the East. The third occurrence of this toponym,
122 72, only tells us that there was a man by the name of
